answer:
**The Falsafa corpus does not contain explicit philosophical or literary analysis defining the trope of *paimaana* (پیمانه, often transliterated as *paimana* or *paimaana*) in Urdu poetry. However, it includes numerous ghazals and nazms from key Urdu poets—Mirza Ghalib, Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, and Allama Iqbal—where the term appears as a standard motif in the Persianate *meykhaana* (wine-house) imagery common to Sufi-influenced ghazal tradition.**[^1][^2]

In these works, *paimaana* literally denotes a "measuring cup" or goblet for wine (*mai* or *sharaab*), often filled to the brim (*labrez*) and etched with a line (*khat-e-paimana*) indicating the measure.[^3][^4] Metaphorically, it symbolizes:
- The vessel of ecstatic, divine intoxication (*nasha*, spiritual enlightenment).
- The allotted portion (*paimaan*) of love, sorrow, or cosmic vision (e.g., echoing the mythical cup of Jamshid, *paimana-e-Jam*, which reveals the world's secrets).[^5]
- Overflowing emotion, like tears (*ashk*) or blood, blurring wine with existential grief.[^6]

**It relates to the *saqi* (ساقی, cupbearer)—typically a beautiful youth, beloved, or divine figure—who pours, offers, or withholds the wine from the *paimaana*.** The *saqi* embodies grace (*lutf*), cruelty (*sitam*), or divine favor, turning the act of serving into a mystical rite. The poet often pleads with or laments the *saqi*, as in direct addresses for more wine or respite from pain.[^7][^8] Together, they evoke the tavern (*maikhana*) as a space of transcendence, where measured wine (*paimaana*) represents life's fleeting, intoxicating quota dispensed by the *saqi*'s hand.[^9]

Examples:
- Ghalib invokes a *paimana-e-sehba* (wine-measuring cup) amid worldly illusion, implying the *saqi*'s role in providing oblivion.[^10]
- Iqbal describes a *paimaana* sufficient "for the whole world" (*saare zamaane ke liye*), tying it to universal longing served by a cosmic *saqi*.[^11]
- Zauq begs the *saqi* endlessly for wine (or soaked kebab), highlighting the trope's playful desperation.[^12]

This imagery draws from pre-Urdu Persian masters like Hafez and Rumi but persists vividly in 19th-century Urdu poetry for themes of love, loss, and Sufi union. For deeper analysis, consult secondary sources on *ghazal* symbolism, as the corpus focuses on primary poetic texts.[^13]

[^1]: *Mirza Ghalib, Diwan-e-Ghalib*, multiple chapters (e.g., ch. 51, 131, 213, 231), [translation/](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/51-gham-nahin-hota-hai-aazaadon-ko-besh-az-yak-nafas/translation/).

[^2]: *Allama Iqbal, Bang-e-Dara* (Parts 1–3), e.g., chs. 8, 18 (Part 1); ch. 4 (Part 3), [transliteration/](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/08-laaon-woh-tinke-kahin-se-aashiyane-ke-liye/transliteration/).

[^3]: Ghalib, ch. 51: "Moo-e-sheesha ko samajhte hain khat-e-paimana hum" (We take the mouth of the glass for the cup's line), [transliteration/#p-c3cb04](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/51-gham-nahin-hota-hai-aazaadon-ko-besh-az-yak-nafas/transliteration/#p-c3cb04).

[^4]: Iqbal, *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, ch. 18: "Jalwa-e-mulk-e-baqa hai khat-e-paimana-e-dil" (The eternal realm's vision is the heart-cup's line), [transliteration/#p-ca079f](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/18-dil/transliteration/#p-ca079f).

[^5]: Ghalib, ch. 208: "Saaf durdi-kash-e-paimana-e-Jam hain hum log" (We are plain Jam's cup scrapers), [transliteration/](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/208-zikr-mera-ba-badi-bhi-usey-manzoor-nahin/transliteration/).

[^6]: Iqbal, *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, ch. 4: "Ashk-e-betaab se labrez hai paimaana tera" (Your cup overflows with restless tears), [transliteration/#p-c80d32](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/transliteration/#p-c80d32).

[^7]: Zauq, *Diwan-e-Zauq*, ch. 58: "kahan talak kahoon saqi ke laa sharaab to de" (How long shall I say to the saqi, bring wine!), [translation/](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/58-kahan-talak-kahoon-saqi-ke-laa-sharaab-to-de/translation/).

[^8]: Iqbal, *Bang-e-Dara Part 2*, ch. 5: "Guzar gaya ab woh daur-e-saqi ke..." (Gone is that age of the saqi's...), [translation/#p-445c2f](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/05-zamana-aaya-hai-be-hijabi-ka-aam-deedaar-e-yaar-ho/translation/#p-445c2f).

[^9]: Ghalib, ch. 50 (Saqi too constrained to ask), [translation/#p-7c819f](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/50-gham-khaane-mein-boda-dil-e-nakaam-bahut-hai/translation/#p-7c819f); ch. 78 (Saqi calls in vain), [translation/#p-62fd33](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/78-husn-ghamze-ki-kashakash-se-chhoota-mere-baad/translation/#p-62fd33).

[^10]: Ghalib, ch. 213: "rakh de koi paimana-e-sehba mere aage" (Place a wine cup before me), [translation/#p-937dd8](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/213-baazeecha-e-atfaal-hai-duniya-mere-aage/translation/#p-937dd8).

[^11]: Iqbal, *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, ch. 8: "Ek paimaana tera saare zamaane ke liye", [transliteration/](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/08-laaon-woh-tinke-kahin-se-aashiyane-ke-liye/transliteration/).

[^12]: Zauq, ch. 58 (full ghazal pleading to saqi), [translation/](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/58-kahan-talak-kahoon-saqi-ke-laa-sharaab-to-de/translation/).

[^13]: Iqbal, *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, ch. 25: "Jam aur Saqi ne bana ki ravish..." (New cup and saqi's ways), [translation/#p-79b1e9](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-79b1e9).

args.query: "wine cup" OR goblet OR "measuring cup" saqi OR cupbearer
